Ophthalmology is the branch of medicine and surgery that deals with the diagnosis and treatment of eye disorders. An 'ophthalmologist' is a medical doctor (MD) who can perform eye exams, prescribe glasses, treat eye diseases, and perform eye surgery.

"An optometrist (OD) is an eye care professional who provides primary vision care. They perform eye exams test vision prescribe glasses and contact lenses and can diagnose and treat certain eye conditions. They are not medical doctors and typically do not perform surgery.Ó

"An orthopedist (or orthopedic surgeon) is a medical doctor who specializes in the musculoskeletal systemŃthe bones joints ligaments tendons and muscles. They treat fractures arthritis sports injuries and other conditions often through surgery.Ó
"Otolaryngology is the medical specialty that deals with conditions of the ear nose and throat (ENT) and related structures of the head and neck.Ó

Urology is the medical and surgical specialty that focuses on the urinary tracts of males and females, and on the reproductive system of males. Urologists treat conditions like UTIs, kidney stones, BPH, and cancers of the urinary tract.

Podiatry is the branch of medicine devoted to the study, diagnosis, and treatment of disorders of the foot, ankle, and lower leg. A 'podiatrist' (DPM) treats conditions like bunions, plantar fasciitis, and diabetic foot problems.

General anesthesia is a state of controlled, temporary unconsciousness that includes a lack of sensation (pain), memory, and movement. It is induced with medications to allow a patient to undergo surgery or other major procedures without distress.

Local anesthesia is a technique that numbs a small, specific part of the body. The patient remains awake and alert, but feels no pain in the area. It is used for minor procedures like getting stitches or having a mole removed. Lidocaine is a common local anesthetic.

Regional anesthesia is a technique used to numb a larger region of the body (like an arm, a leg, or the lower half of the body). The patient remains awake. An 'epidural' (used in childbirth) and a 'spinal block' are common types of regional anesthesia.

A Foley catheter is a flexible tube that is passed through the urethra and into the bladder to drain urine. It is often used during surgery, in patients who are incontinent, or in those who cannot empty their bladder (urinary retention).
Atrophy is the decrease in size or wasting away of a body part or tissue. 'Muscle atrophy' is the shrinking of muscles, often from disuse (like being in a cast) or from a neurological disease.

Hypertrophy is the increase in the size of an organ or tissue due to the increase in the size of its cells. 'Muscle hypertrophy' is the growth of muscle cells from exercise. 'Cardiac hypertrophy' is an abnormal, unhealthy thickening of the heart muscle.
Stenosis is the medical term for the abnormal narrowing of a passage in the body. 'Spinal stenosis' is a narrowing of the spinal canal. 'Aortic stenosis' is a narrowing of the heart's aortic valve.

Ischemia is a condition in which there is a restriction in blood supply to a tissue, causing a shortage of oxygen. 'Myocardial ischemia' is reduced blood flow to the heart muscle (causing angina). If it's severe or prolonged, it leads to 'infarction' (tissue death).

Infarction is the obstruction of the blood supply to an organ or region of tissue, causing local death (necrosis) of the tissue. A 'myocardial infarction' is a heart attack. A 'cerebral infarction' is an ischemic stroke.
Necrosis is the death of most or all of the cells in an organ or tissue due to disease, injury, or failure of the blood supply. 'Avascular necrosis' is bone death caused by a loss of blood supply.
Idiopathic is a term used when the cause of a disease or condition is unknown. For example, 'idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis' is scarring of the lungs without a known cause.

A congenital disorder (or congenital anomaly) is a condition that is present at birth. It can be inherited (genetic) or caused by environmental factors during pregnancy. A 'congenital heart defect' is a structural problem with the heart present at birth.

An iatrogenic condition is a disease, complication, or side effect that is caused by medical treatment or a medical provider. For example, a 'nosocomial' (hospital-acquired) infection is an iatrogenic condition.

'Acute' describes a condition that is of sudden onset, sharp, and severe, but often of short duration (e.g., acute appendicitis). 'Chronic' describes a condition that develops slowly and persists for a long time, often for life (e.g., chronic diabetes).

A prognosis is a doctor's forecast of the likely course and outcome of a disease. It's an educated guess about a patient's chances of recovery, based on the disease, their age, their overall health, and the treatment options.

'Curative' treatment aims to cure a disease and completely remove it. 'Palliative' treatment aims to relieve the symptoms and suffering caused by a serious illness and to improve quality of life, without necessarily curing the underlying disease.
Remission is a period during which the symptoms of a chronic illness (like cancer or an autoimmune disease) are partially or completely gone. It does not necessarily mean the disease has been cured.
A relapse is the return of a disease or its symptoms after a period of remission or improvement.

Adherence (or compliance) is the extent to which a patient follows their doctor's medical advice. This most often refers to 'medication adherence'Ńwhether the patient takes their medication as prescribed (right dose, right time, right duration).

Titration is the process of adjusting the dose of a medication to find the best, most effective dose for a patient while minimizing side effects. This often involves starting at a low dose and slowly increasing it over time.

A loading dose is a large, initial dose of a drug given to quickly achieve a therapeutic concentration in the bloodstream. It's often followed by smaller 'maintenance doses' to keep the drug level steady.

A black box warning (or 'boxed warning') is the most serious warning required by the FDA on a prescription drug's label. It's used to call attention to serious or life-threatening risks, such as addiction (for opioids) or severe side effects.

'Off-label' prescribing is when a doctor prescribes a drug for a different purpose, in a different dose, or for a different patient group than what the FDA has officially approved it for. This is legal and common, but is based on the doctor's professional judgment.

Escherichia coli (E. coli) is a type of bacteria that normally lives in the intestines of healthy people and animals. Most strains are harmless, but some, like E. coli O157:H7, can cause severe food poisoning, resulting in bloody diarrhea and kidney failure. It is also the most common cause of UTIs.

Salmonella is a common type of bacteria that causes food poisoning (salmonellosis). It's typically found in raw poultry, eggs, and unwashed fruit. Symptoms include diarrhea, fever, and abdominal cramps.

Listeria is a type of bacteria found in soil, water, and some animals. It can cause a serious foodborne illness called listeriosis, especially in pregnant women, newborns, and immunocompromised people. It's often found in unpasteurized milk, deli meats, and soft cheeses.

'Staph aureus' is a common bacterium found on the skin and in the nose of many healthy people. It's a common cause of skin infections (like boils), but can also cause serious infections like pneumonia and sepsis if it enters the bloodstream. MRSA is a resistant strain.

Group A Strep is a bacterium that causes a variety of illnesses. It is the cause of 'strep throat' and skin infections like impetigo. In rare, severe cases, it can cause 'necrotizing fasciitis' (flesh-eating disease) or toxic shock syndrome.

Rheumatic fever is a rare but serious inflammatory disease that can develop as a complication of untreated or inadequately treated strep throat. It can cause permanent damage to the heart valves (rheumatic heart disease).

Tuberculosis is a serious infectious disease that mainly affects the lungs, caused by the bacterium *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. It spreads through the air when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can be 'latent' (no symptoms) or 'active' (causing illness).

Malaria is a life-threatening disease caused by *Plasmodium* parasites, which are transmitted to people through the bites of infected female *Anopheles* mosquitoes. It's common in tropical regions and causes high fevers, chills, and flu-like illness.

Rabies is a deadly viral disease that attacks the central nervous system. It's transmitted to humans from the saliva of infected animals, usually through a bite (e.g., from a bat, raccoon, or dog). Once symptoms appear, it is nearly 100% fatal, but it is preventable with a vaccine given *after* exposure.

Tetanus ('lockjaw') is a serious bacterial infection that affects the nervous system, causing painful muscle contractions, particularly of the jaw and neck. The bacteria (*Clostridium tetani*) are found in soil and enter the body through a deep cut or puncture wound. It is preventable with a vaccine (DTaP, Tdap).

Lyme disease is a bacterial infection transmitted to humans through the bite of an infected blacklegged tick (deer tick). The classic early symptom is a 'bull's-eye' rash (erythema migrans), followed by fever, fatigue, and joint pain. If untreated, it can spread to the heart, joints, and nervous system.

*Helicobacter pylori* (H. pylori) is a type of bacteria that infects the stomach. It is a very common cause of peptic ulcers (sores in the lining of the stomach or small intestine) and is also a primary risk factor for stomach cancer.

A peptic ulcer is an open sore that develops on the inside lining of the stomach (gastric ulcer) or the upper portion of the small intestine (duodenal ulcer). The most common causes are infection with *H. pylori* bacteria and long-term use of NSAIDs.

Zika virus is a disease spread primarily through the bite of an infected *Aedes* species mosquito. While it often causes no or mild symptoms, infection during pregnancy can cause a severe birth defect called 'microcephaly' (abnormally small head) and other brain defects.

Ebola is a rare but severe and often fatal viral hemorrhagic fever that occurs in humans and other primates. It spreads through direct contact with the bodily fluids of an infected person or animal. It causes high fever, severe bleeding, and organ failure.

Norovirus is a highly contagious virus that is the most common cause of gastroenteritis ('stomach flu') in the U.S. It spreads easily through contaminated food, water, or surfaces (like on cruise ships). It causes severe vomiting and diarrhea, but usually resolves in 1-3 days.

Rotavirus is a highly contagious virus that is the most common cause of severe, watery diarrhea and vomiting in infants and young children worldwide. A vaccine is now routinely given to babies to prevent this.

Influenza is a contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses. It can cause mild to severe illness, with symptoms like fever, cough, sore throat, body aches, and fatigue. It is different from the 'stomach flu' (gastroenteritis). An annual flu vaccine is the best prevention.

The common cold is a viral infection of the upper respiratory tract (nose and throat). It's typically harmless, although it may not feel that way. Symptoms include a runny nose, sore throat, and cough. Many different viruses can cause a cold, but rhinoviruses are the most common.

Sinusitis is inflammation of the sinuses, which are the air-filled cavities around the nasal passages. It's often called a 'sinus infection.' It causes a stuffy nose, headache, and pain/pressure in the face. It can be acute (often viral) or chronic.

Bronchitis is inflammation of the lining of your bronchial tubes, which carry air to and from your lungs. 'Acute bronchitis' is very common, often develops from a cold, and causes a nagging cough. 'Chronic bronchitis' is a form of COPD.
Laryngitis is inflammation of your voice box (larynx) from overuse, irritation, or infection. This causes your vocal cords to swell, resulting in a hoarse, raspy voice or complete loss of your voice.

Pharyngitis is the medical term for inflammation of the pharynx, which is the back of the throat. It's the technical term for a 'sore throat.' It's most often caused by a virus (like the common cold) but can be caused by bacteria (like strep throat).

Otitis media is the medical term for a middle ear infection. It's very common in children and occurs when the area behind the eardrum becomes inflamed and infected, often after a cold. It causes ear pain and fever.

Otitis externa is the medical term for 'swimmer's ear.' It is an infection of the outer ear canal, which runs from your eardrum to the outside of your head. It's often caused by water remaining in the ear, creating a moist environment for bacteria to grow.

Conjunctivitis is inflammation or infection of the conjunctiva, the transparent membrane that lines the eyelid and covers the white part of the eyeball. It can be viral, bacterial (both highly contagious), or allergic. It causes redness, itching, and discharge.
A stye is a painful, red, pus-filled lump that grows on the edge of the eyelid. It's an infection of an eyelash follicle, similar to a pimple.

Dermatitis is a general term for inflammation of the skin. It has many causes and forms and usually involves an itchy rash on swollen, reddened skin. 'Eczema' (atopic dermatitis) and 'contact dermatitis' (from poison ivy or an-allergen) are common types.

Eczema is a chronic, inflammatory skin condition that causes dry, red, intensely itchy patches of skin. The exact cause is unknown but is related to a combination of genetics, immune system dysfunction, and environmental triggers.

Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune disease that causes the rapid overproduction of skin cells. This leads to scaling on the skin's surface, forming thick, silvery-white, itchy, and sometimes painful plaques.

Acne is a very common skin condition that occurs when hair follicles become plugged with oil (sebum) and dead skin cells. This allows bacteria to grow, leading to inflammation and lesions (pimples, blackheads, whiteheads).

Rosacea is a common, chronic skin condition that causes persistent redness, blushing, or flushing and visible blood vessels in the face. It can also produce small, red, pus-filled bumps (acne rosacea).

Melasma is a common skin problem that causes dark, discolored patches (hyperpigmentation) on the skin, typically on the face. It's often triggered by hormonal changes (like pregnancy, 'the mask of pregnancy') and sun exposure.
Vitiligo is an autoimmune disease that causes the loss of skin color in patches. It occurs when melanocytes (the cells that produce pigment) are destroyed. It can affect skin anywhere on the body.

Alopecia is the general medical term for hair loss. 'Alopecia areata' is an autoimmune disease that causes hair to fall out in small, round patches. 'Androgenetic alopecia' is male- or female-pattern baldness.
A wart is a small, rough, fleshy growth on the skin caused by an infection with a type of human papillomavirus (HPV). They are contagious and most common on the hands or feet (plantar warts).

Athlete's foot is a common contagious fungal infection of the skin on the feet. It's a type of 'ringworm.' It thrives in warm, moist environments (like sweaty socks) and causes an itchy, scaly rash, often between the toes.
Ringworm is *not* caused by a worm. It is a common fungal skin infection (a 'tinea' infection) that causes a characteristic red, scaly, itchy, ring-shaped rash on the body. It is contagious.

Impetigo is a highly contagious bacterial skin infection, most common in young children. It's caused by Staph or Strep bacteria and results in red sores or blisters that rupture and form honey-colored crusts, usually around the nose and mouth.

Cellulitis is a common, and potentially serious, bacterial infection of the skin and the tissues beneath it. It's *not* contagious. It occurs when bacteria (usually Staph or Strep) enter through a break in the skin. The area becomes red, swollen, warm, and painful.

MRSA (Methicillin-resistant *Staphylococcus aureus*) is a 'staph' bacteria that is resistant to many antibiotics. It often starts as a skin infection, looking like a painful, swollen, red bump (like a spider bite or boil), and can quickly become a deep, painful abscess.

A skin abscess (or boil) is a localized collection of pus that has built up within the tissue of the body. It's a sign of infection, and the body walls off the pus. It appears as a painful, swollen lump that is warm to the touch. It often needs to be drained by a doctor.

Scabies is an intensely itchy skin infestation caused by a tiny, burrowing mite (*Sarcoptes scabiei*). The mite burrows into the top layer of skin to lay its eggs, causing an allergic reaction and a pimple-like rash. It's highly contagious and spreads through prolonged skin-to-skin contact.

Hives are an outbreak of swollen, pale red or skin-colored welts (wheals) on the skin that appear suddenly as part of an allergic reaction or for unknown reasons. They are often very itchy and can 'blanch' (turn white) when pressed.

Angioedema is swelling in the deep layers of the skin, often seen with hives. It typically affects the face, lips, tongue, and throat. Swelling of the throat is a medical emergency as it can block the airway.

"The Heimlich maneuver (or 'abdominal thrusts') is a first-aid procedure used to treat choking. It's performed by standing behind the choking person and using your hands to exert pressure on the bottom of their diaphragm forcing air out of the lungs to dislodge the object.Ó

An AED (Automated External Defibrillator) is a device used to shock a heart in sudden cardiac arrest. Turn it on, and it will give you voice prompts. Place the sticky pads on the patient's bare chest as shown in the diagram. The AED will analyze the heart rhythm and tell you if a shock is needed. If so, it will tell you to 'stand clear' and press the 'shock' button.

Anaphylactic shock is the most severe form of anaphylaxis, a life-threatening allergic reaction. It causes a massive release of histamine, leading to widespread vasodilation (blood vessels widening), a sudden, severe drop in blood pressure, and airway constriction. It is fatal if not treated immediately with epinephrine.

query: What are the signs of a 'concussion'?
reference_answer: Signs and symptoms include headache, temporary loss of consciousness, confusion or 'fogginess,' dizziness, ringing in the ears (tinnitus), nausea, and vomiting. Symptoms can be delayed for hours or days.

Have the person sit down and lean *forward* (not back, to avoid swallowing blood). Pinch the soft, fleshy part of the nose (nostrils) firmly shut. Hold this pressure continuously for at least 10-15 minutes. A cold compress on the bridge of the nose can also help.

query: How do you treat a 'minor burn' (first-degree)?
reference_answer: Immediately run cool (not cold or ice) water over the burn for 10-15 minutes. After cooling, you can cover it with a sterile, non-stick bandage. You can take an over-the-counter pain reliever like ibuprofen.

query: How do you treat a 'severe burn' (third-degree)?
reference_answer: Call 911 immediately. This is a medical emergency. Do *not* run cold water over a large, severe burn, as it can cause hypothermia. Do *not* remove clothing that is stuck to the burn. Cover the area loosely with a cool, moist, sterile bandage or clean cloth.
Signs include heavy sweating, cold, pale, and clammy skin, a fast but weak pulse, nausea, muscle cramps, dizziness, and headache. This is a precursor to heatstroke.
Move the person to a cool place. Loosen their clothing. Have them sip cool water. Apply cool, wet cloths to their body or give them a cool bath.
This is a medical emergency. Signs include a very high body temperature (103ˇF+), hot, red, and *dry* skin (no sweating), a fast and strong pulse, confusion, and loss of consciousness.
Call 911 immediately. Move the person to a cooler environment and try to cool them down rapidly with cool cloths or a cool bath. Do *not* give them anything to drink.
Signs include intense shivering (which may stop as it worsens), slurred speech, confusion, drowsiness, and slow, shallow breathing.
Frostbite is a freezing injury to skin and underlying tissues. The skin becomes very cold, white or grayish-yellow, firm or waxy, and numb.

Get the person to a warm place. Do *not* rub the frostbitten area, as this can cause more damage. Gradually rewarm the area using warm (not hot) water, or body heat. Do not use direct heat like a fire or heating pad.

"Antivenom (or antivenin) is a biological treatment for a venomous bite or sting (e.g. from a snake or spider). It's created by immunizing an animal (like a horse) with small amounts of the venom and then harvesting the antibodies their blood produces. These antibodies are given to the patient to neutralize the venom.Ó

Loperamide is an over-the-counter medication used to treat diarrhea. It works by slowing down the movement of the gut, which allows more water to be absorbed from the stool, making it less watery and less frequent.
Dextromethorphan (often 'DM' or 'DXM' in product names) is an over-the-counter cough suppressant. It works by affecting the signals in the brain that trigger the cough reflex.
Guaifenesin is an 'expectorant.' It's an over-the-counter medication used to treat chest congestion. It works by thinning and loosening mucus in the airways, making it easier to cough up.

Pseudoephedrine is a 'decongestant' used to relieve nasal and sinus congestion. It works by shrinking swollen blood vessels in the nasal passages. It is kept 'behind the counter' in pharmacies because it can be used to illegally make methamphetamine.

Phenylephrine is an over-the-counter 'decongestant' used to relieve nasal stuffiness. It is the common replacement for pseudoephedrine in 'front-of-counter' cold medicines, though its effectiveness as an oral decongestant is widely debated.

Diphenhydramine is a first-generation 'antihistamine.' It is used to treat allergy symptoms, hives, and insomnia. Its primary side effect is significant drowsiness, which is why it's also the active ingredient in sleeping aids like ZzzQuil.

Loratadine is a second-generation 'antihistamine.' It is used to treat allergy symptoms (runny nose, itchy eyes) but is 'non-drowsy' because it does not cross the blood-brain barrier as readily as first-generation antihistamines.

Cetirizine is a second-generation 'antihistamine' used to treat allergy symptoms. It is generally considered 'non-drowsy' but can cause drowsiness in a small percentage of people, more so than loratadine.

Fexofenadine is a second-generation 'antihistamine' used to treat allergy symptoms. It is known for being non-drowsy. Its absorption can be significantly reduced by fruit juices like grapefruit, orange, and apple juice.

Ranitidine was a very popular 'H2 blocker' medication used to reduce stomach acid and treat heartburn and GERD. It was recalled and removed from the market worldwide in 2020 due to contamination with a probable carcinogen called NDMA.

Famotidine is an 'H2 blocker' medication. It works by decreasing the amount of acid the stomach produces. It is used to treat and prevent heartburn, GERD, and stomach ulcers. It became more popular after the recall of ranitidine.

Omeprazole is a 'proton-pump inhibitor' (PPI). It's a very common medication used to treat frequent heartburn, GERD, and ulcers. It works by blocking the 'pumps' in the stomach that produce acid, resulting in a stronger and longer-lasting acid reduction than H2 blockers.

query: What are common side effects of long-term 'PPI' use?
reference_answer: Long-term use of Proton-Pump Inhibitors (like omeprazole) has been linked to potential risks, including an increased risk of bone fractures (due to reduced calcium absorption), C. difficile infection, and low magnesium and vitamin B12 levels.

Calcium carbonate is an 'antacid.' It works by directly neutralizing stomach acid on contact, providing very fast, but temporary, relief from heartburn and indigestion. It is also used as a calcium supplement.

Simethicone is an over-the-counter medication used to relieve the symptoms of excess gas in the stomach and intestines, such as bloating, pressure, and fullness. It works by breaking up gas bubbles, making them easier to pass.

Psyllium husk is a type of 'bulk-forming' soluble fiber. It's used as a supplement to treat constipation and promote regularity. It works by absorbing water in the gut to make stools softer and easier to pass.

Docusate sodium is a 'stool softener.' It works by drawing water and fats into the stool, which softens it and makes it easier to pass. It is used to prevent constipation (e.g., after surgery), but not to treat existing, hard constipation.

Bisacodyl is a 'stimulant laxative.' It works by directly stimulating the muscles of the intestine, causing them to contract and move stool along. It is effective for treating constipation but is intended for short-term use, as long-term use can lead to dependence.

MiraLAX is an 'osmotic laxative.' It works by pulling water *into* the colon, which softens the stool and increases the frequency of bowel movements. It is generally considered safe for longer-term use than stimulant laxatives.
Ketoconazole is an 'antifungal' medication. It is used to treat fungal infections. As a prescription shampoo, it is used to treat seborrheic dermatitis (dandruff).
Miconazole is an 'antifungal' medication. It is most commonly used as an over-the-counter topical cream or suppository to treat vaginal yeast infections (candidiasis).
Clotrimazole is an 'antifungal' medication. It is used topically to treat skin infections like athlete's foot, ringworm, and jock itch, as well as vaginal yeast infections.

Terbinafine is an 'antifungal' medication. It's used as a topical cream for athlete's foot, but it's most known as an oral (pill) medication used to treat fungal infections of the fingernails and toenails, which requires months of treatment.

Acyclovir is an 'antiviral' medication. It's used to treat infections caused by the herpes virus, including cold sores (herpes labialis), genital herpes, and shingles (herpes zoster). It does not cure herpes, but it helps sores heal faster and reduces the frequency of outbreaks.

Oseltamivir is an 'antiviral' medication used to treat and, in some cases, prevent influenza (the flu). It works best if started within 48 hours of the first symptoms. It can shorten the duration of the flu and reduce the severity of symptoms.

Amoxicillin is a very common 'penicillin-class' antibiotic. It's used to treat a wide variety of bacterial infections, such as strep throat, ear infections, and pneumonia. It is *not* effective against viruses like the cold or flu.

Augmentin is a combination antibiotic. It contains amoxicillin plus clavulanate. The clavulanate is a 'beta-lactamase inhibitor' that stops bacteria from destroying the amoxicillin, making the drug effective against a wider range of resistant bacteria.

Azithromycin is a 'macrolide' antibiotic. It's often prescribed as a 5-day 'Z-Pak' to treat bacterial infections like bronchitis, pneumonia, and sinus infections. It has a long half-life, so it keeps working in the body for days after the last dose.

Ciprofloxacin is a 'fluoroquinolone' antibiotic. It's a broad-spectrum antibiotic used to treat serious bacterial infections, including UTIs, kidney infections, and bacterial pneumonia. It carries a black box warning for risk of tendon rupture.

Levofloxacin is a 'fluoroquinolone' antibiotic, similar to ciprofloxacin. It's used to treat severe or resistant bacterial infections like pneumonia and complicated UTIs. It also carries a black box warning for risk of tendon rupture and nerve damage.

Doxycycline is a 'tetracycline' antibiotic. It's a very broad-spectrum antibiotic used to treat a wide range of infections, including bacterial pneumonia, acne, and tick-borne diseases like Lyme disease. It should not be taken with dairy and can cause severe sun sensitivity.

Metronidazole is an antibiotic and antiprotozoal medication. It's uniquely effective against anaerobic bacteria and certain parasites. It's used to treat infections like C. difficile, bacterial vaginosis, and giardia. Patients must *never* drink alcohol while taking it, as it causes a severe, violent vomiting reaction.

Bactrim is a 'sulfa' combination antibiotic. It's very commonly used to treat UTIs, MRSA skin infections, and certain types of pneumonia. Patients must drink a lot of water with it and can have severe allergic reactions if they have a 'sulfa allergy.'

Vancomycin is a powerful, 'glycopeptide' antibiotic. It is typically given intravenously (IV) in the hospital to treat severe infections, including MRSA in the bloodstream. An oral (pill) form is used *only* to treat C. difficile infection in the colon (as it's not absorbed).

Clindamycin is a 'lincosamide' antibiotic. It is effective against a wide range of bacteria, including some MRSA. It's often used for skin, dental, or bone infections. Its most notorious side effect is a high risk of causing a severe C. difficile infection.

Prednisone is a 'corticosteroid' (steroid) medication. It's a powerful anti-inflammatory and immune-suppressing drug. It's used for a huge variety of conditions, such as severe asthma attacks, poison ivy, autoimmune flares (like lupus), and to prevent organ transplant rejection.

query: What are common side effects of 'prednisone' (short-term)?
reference_answer: Even in the short term (like a 5-day 'dose pack'), prednisone can cause significant side effects, including insomnia ('roid rage'), increased appetite, fluid retention (bloating), high blood sugar, and mood changes or agitation.

Topical hydrocortisone is a low-potency 'corticosteroid' cream or ointment. It's available over-the-counter (e.g., Cortizone-10) and is used to relieve the itching and inflammation from minor skin irritations, insect bites, and eczema.

Codeine is an 'opioid' (narcotic) medication. It is used to treat mild to moderate pain and also as a cough suppressant. It's often combined with acetaminophen (Tylenol #3). It is less potent than other opioids but still carries a risk of addiction.

Hydrocodone is a powerful 'opioid' (narcotic) pain reliever. It is almost always combined with acetaminophen (e.g., Vicodin, Norco). It is used to treat severe pain and carries a very high risk of addiction and respiratory depression.

Oxycodone is a powerful 'opioid' (narcotic) pain reliever. It is used to treat severe pain. It is available as an immediate-release tablet (e.g., Percocet, when combined with acetaminophen) and as a long-acting, extended-release tablet (OxyContin). It has a very high risk of addiction.

Morphine is a potent 'opioid' (narcotic) pain medication. It is one of the oldest and most important pain relievers, used to treat severe acute and chronic pain (e.g., in cancer patients or after surgery). It is the benchmark against which other opioids are measured.

Fentanyl is an extremely potent synthetic 'opioid' (narcotic) pain reliever. It is 50-100 times more potent than morphine. In medicine, it's used in hospitals for anesthesia and to manage severe pain (e.g., via a transdermal patch). Illegally, it is responsible for a massive number of overdose deaths.

Naloxone (brand name Narcan) is an 'opioid antagonist.' It is a life-saving emergency medication used to rapidly reverse an opioid overdose. It works by binding to opioid receptors, blocking the effects of the opioids and restoring breathing in someone who has overdosed.

Methadone is a long-acting synthetic 'opioid.' It is used for two main purposes: to treat severe chronic pain, and as a 'medication-assisted treatment' (MAT) for opioid use disorder. In MAT, it reduces cravings and withdrawal symptoms, allowing a person to stabilize their life.

Buprenorphine is a 'partial opioid agonist.' It's used to treat opioid use disorder. It relieves withdrawal and cravings without producing the same 'high' as other opioids, making it a key part of 'medication-assisted treatment' (MAT). It's often combined with naloxone (as Suboxone) to deter misuse.

Tramadol is a synthetic pain medication. It has a 'dual mechanism'Ńit's a weak opioid agonist *and* it increases serotonin and norepinephrine (like an SNRI). It's used for moderate pain. It was once thought to be safer, but it still carries a risk of addiction and seizures.

Ibuprofen is a very common 'NSAID' (Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drug). It's used to reduce pain, fever, and inflammation. It's used for headaches, menstrual cramps, and arthritis. It can cause stomach upset and should not be taken on an empty stomach.
Naproxen is an 'NSAID,' similar to ibuprofen. Its main advantage is that it lasts longer, so it only needs to be taken every 8-12 hours, whereas ibuprofen is taken every 4-6 hours.

Acetaminophen is *not* an NSAID. It is a very common over-the-counter medication used to reduce pain and fever. It does *not* reduce inflammation. It is safe for the stomach, but a large overdose can cause severe and fatal liver damage.

Taking acetaminophen (Tylenol) with alcoholŃespecially chronic, heavy alcohol useŃcan be very dangerous. Both substances are processed by the liver. Combining them, or taking Tylenol for a hangover, overwhelms the liver's metabolic pathways and can lead to severe, acute liver failure.

Reye's syndrome is a rare but extremely serious condition causing swelling in the brain and liver. It most often affects children and teens recovering from a viral infection (like the flu or chickenpox). It has a strong link to taking aspirin, which is why children should *never* be given aspirin for a fever.

Aspirin is an 'NSAID.' It's used for pain, fever, and inflammation. Its most unique use is as a 'blood thinner' (an anti-platelet drug). A 'baby aspirin' (low-dose, 81mg) is taken daily by many people to prevent heart attacks and strokes by stopping platelets from clumping.

Clopidogrel is an 'anti-platelet' medication (a blood thinner), similar to aspirin but more potent. It's used to prevent blood clots in people who have had a heart attack or stroke, or who have a 'stent' placed in a coronary artery.

A stent is a tiny, expandable mesh tube. In cardiology, it's placed inside a blocked or narrowed coronary artery during an 'angioplasty.' The stent is expanded, pushing the plaque against the artery wall and holding the artery open to restore blood flow to the heart.

Angioplasty is a minimally invasive procedure used to open blocked coronary arteries. A thin tube (catheter) with a balloon on its tip is guided to the blockage. The balloon is then inflated, which compresses the plaque and widens the artery. A stent is almost always placed at the same time.

CABG (pronounced 'cabbage') is 'bypass surgery.' It's an open-heart surgery for severe coronary artery disease. A surgeon takes a healthy blood vessel (a 'graft') from another part of the body (like the leg or chest) and uses it to create a new pathŃa 'bypass'Ńaround the blocked section of the coronary artery.

Warfarin is an 'anticoagulant' (blood thinner) medication. It's used to prevent blood clots in conditions like atrial fibrillation or after a deep vein thrombosis (DVT). It works by blocking the action of Vitamin K, which is essential for clotting. Patients require regular blood tests (INR) to monitor it.

The INR (International Normalized Ratio) is a blood test used to measure how long it takes for blood to clot. It is the standard test used to monitor the effectiveness of the anticoagulant drug warfarin (Coumadin) and to ensure the dose is both safe and effective.

query: Why must 'warfarin' users watch their 'Vitamin K' intake?
reference_answer: Warfarin works by blocking Vitamin K, which is used to make clotting factors. Eating foods high in Vitamin K (like spinach, kale, and other leafy greens) can counteract the drug, making it less effective and increasing clot risk. Patients must keep their Vitamin K intake *consistent* from day to day.

Heparin is a powerful, fast-acting 'anticoagulant' (blood thinner). It is given by injection (IV or subcutaneous) in the hospital to treat and prevent blood clots, such as DVT or pulmonary embolism, or during surgery.

Enoxaparin is a 'low-molecular-weight heparin' (LMWH). It's an anticoagulant (blood thinner) given as a subcutaneous injection. It's often used as a 'bridge' therapy when starting warfarin or for preventing DVTs in high-risk patients (like after a hip replacement).

Rivaroxaban is a 'direct oral anticoagulant' (DOAC). It's a newer type of blood thinner used to prevent and treat blood clots (like in A-Fib or DVT). Unlike warfarin, it works by directly blocking a clotting factor (Factor Xa) and does *not* require routine INR blood monitoring.

Apixaban is a 'direct oral anticoagulant' (DOAC), similar to rivaroxaban. It's a blood thinner that directly blocks Factor Xa and is used to prevent strokes in atrial fibrillation and to treat DVT/PE. It also does not require INR monitoring.

Atropine is a medication used to treat certain types of slow heart rate (bradycardia), especially in an emergency. It's an 'anticholinergic' drug that works by blocking the vagus nerve, which increases the heart rate. It's also used to dilate pupils for eye exams.

Epinephrine is a hormone and medication. As a drug, it's used in many emergencies. It's the primary treatment for anaphylaxis (in an EpiPen). It's used in cardiac arrest (during CPR) to restart the heart. It's also a powerful 'vasopressor' to raise critically low blood pressure.

As a medication, dopamine is a 'vasopressor' given by IV in an intensive care unit (ICU). It's used to treat critically low blood pressure (shock) and certain types of heart failure by increasing the heart's pumping strength and constricting blood vessels.

Norepinephrine (brand name Levophed) is the first-choice 'vasopressor' medication for treating septic shock. It is given by IV in an ICU to powerfully constrict blood vessels, which raises a dangerously low blood pressure to maintain blood flow to vital organs.

Nitroglycerin is a 'vasodilator' medication. It's used to treat angina (chest pain). It works by relaxing and widening blood vessels, which improves blood flow to the heart muscle and reduces its workload. It's often given as a small tablet dissolved under the tongue for rapid relief.

Furosemide is a powerful 'loop diuretic' (or 'water pill'). It's used to treat fluid overload (edema) caused by heart failure, liver disease, or kidney disease. It works by making the kidneys excrete large amounts of salt and water in the urine.

Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) is a 'thiazide diuretic' (or 'water pill'). It's one of the most common medications used as a first-line treatment for high blood pressure (hypertension). It works by helping the kidneys remove salt and water from the body.

Spironolactone is a 'potassium-sparing diuretic.' Unlike other diuretics that cause you to lose potassium, this one helps your body retain it. It's used to treat high blood pressure and heart failure, and also has anti-androgen effects used to treat PCOS.

Lisinopril is a very common 'ACE inhibitor' medication. It's a first-line drug for treating high blood pressure and heart failure. It works by relaxing blood vessels. A common, peculiar side effect is a persistent, dry cough.

Losartan is an 'ARB' (Angiotensin II Receptor Blocker). It's used to treat high blood pressure and heart failure, similar to ACE inhibitors. It's often prescribed to patients who cannot tolerate the 'ACE cough' caused by drugs like lisinopril.

Metoprolol is a 'beta-blocker' medication. It's one of the most widely used drugs for treating high blood pressure, angina, and heart failure. It works by slowing the heart rate and reducing its pumping force.

Carvedilol is a 'beta-blocker' with an additional 'alpha-blocking' effect. This means it not only slows the heart rate (beta) but also widens blood vessels (alpha), making it particularly effective for treating heart failure.

Amlodipine is a 'calcium channel blocker' medication. It's a very common drug used to treat high blood pressure and angina. It works by relaxing and widening blood vessels. A common side effect is swelling in the ankles (edema).

Diltiazem is a 'calcium channel blocker.' Unlike amlodipine, it not only widens blood vessels but also slows the heart rate. This makes it useful for treating high blood pressure, angina, and also for controlling a rapid heart rate in conditions like atrial fibrillation.
Atorvastatin is a 'statin' medication. It's one of the most prescribed drugs in the world. It's used to lower 'bad' LDL cholesterol levels in the blood, which helps prevent heart attacks and strokes.
Simvastatin is a 'statin' medication, similar to atorvastatin. It's used to lower high cholesterol and reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease.

Ezetimibe is a non-statin cholesterol medication. It works by a different mechanism: it blocks the absorption of cholesterol from food in the small intestine. It's often prescribed *with* a statin to further lower LDL levels.
Gemfibrozil is a 'fibrate' medication. Unlike statins (which target LDL), fibrates are primarily used to lower high 'triglyceride' levels in the blood.

Metformin is the first-line oral medication used to treat Type 2 diabetes. It works by decreasing the liver's production of sugar (glucose) and increasing the body's sensitivity to insulin. It does *not* cause low blood sugar (hypoglycemia) by itself.

Glipizide is a 'sulfonylurea' medication for Type 2 diabetes. It works by stimulating the pancreas to release more insulin. Because it forces insulin release, it carries a high risk of causing 'hypoglycemia' (low blood sugar).
Pioglitazone is a 'TZD' medication for Type 2 diabetes. It works by making the body's tissues more sensitive to insulin. It carries a black box warning for causing or worsening heart failure.

Liraglutide is a 'GLP-1 agonist' medication. It's an injectable (non-insulin) drug for Type 2 diabetes that helps release insulin, slows digestion, and reduces appetite. Under the brand name Saxenda, it's also approved for weight loss.

Semaglutide is a 'GLP-1 agonist,' similar to liraglutide. As Ozempic, it's used for Type 2 diabetes. As Wegovy (a higher dose), it's a highly effective medication for chronic weight management (weight loss).
Sitagliptin is a 'DPP-4 inhibitor' medication for Type 2 diabetes. It's an oral pill that works by increasing levels of hormones that signal the pancreas to release insulin after a meal.

Empagliflozin is an 'SGLT2 inhibitor' medication. It's an oral pill for Type 2 diabetes that works in a unique way: it forces the kidneys to excrete excess sugar (glucose) into the urine. It has also been shown to have major benefits for heart failure.

Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas that allows your body to use sugar (glucose) from food for energy. In medicine, it's a vital, life-saving medication for all people with Type 1 diabetes and many with Type 2 diabetes.

Long-acting (or 'basal') insulin is a type of insulin that is injected once or twice a day. It provides a slow, steady release of insulin over 24 hours to control blood sugar between meals and overnight.
Rapid-acting (or 'bolus') insulin is a type of insulin taken just before or with a meal. It works very quickly (within 15 minutes) to cover the rise in blood sugar from the carbohydrates in the food.

Hypoglycemia is the medical term for low blood sugar. It is a common and dangerous side effect of insulin and other diabetes drugs (like sulfonylureas). Symptoms include shakiness, sweating, confusion, and palpitations. If severe, it can cause seizures or loss of consciousness.

"Glucagon is a hormone that does the *opposite* of insulin: it raises blood sugar by signaling the liver to release stored glucose. In medicine a 'glucagon emergency kit' is an injection given to a person with diabetes who is experiencing a severe hypoglycemic episode (e.g. has passed out) to rapidly raise their blood sugar.Ó

Levothyroxine is a synthetic thyroid hormone. It is one of the most prescribed drugs in the world. It is a replacement hormone used to treat 'hypothyroidism' (underactive thyroid). Patients must take it on an empty stomach, first thing in the morning.

Methimazole is an 'antithyroid' medication. It is used to treat 'hyperthyroidism' (overactive thyroid, e.g., Graves' disease). It works by blocking the thyroid gland from producing new thyroid hormone.

Allopurinol is the most common medication used for the *prevention* of 'gout' attacks. It is *not* a pain reliever. It works by reducing the body's production of uric acid, the substance that forms painful crystals in the joints.
Colchicine is an anti-inflammatory medication used to *treat* an acute, painful gout attack. It can also be used in low doses to prevent attacks.

Tamsulosin is an 'alpha-blocker' medication. It's used almost exclusively in men to treat the urinary symptoms of an enlarged prostate (BPH). It works by relaxing the muscles in the prostate and bladder neck, making it easier to urinate.

Finasteride is a '5-alpha-reductase inhibitor.' As Proscar, it's used to treat an enlarged prostate (BPH) by *shrinking* the prostate. As Propecia (a much lower dose), it's used to treat male-pattern baldness. Pregnant women must not even handle the pills, as it can cause birth defects.
Sertraline is an 'SSRI' (Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor). It's a very common antidepressant used to treat depression, anxiety disorders, OCD, and PTSD.

Fluoxetine is an 'SSRI' antidepressant. It was one of the first SSRIs and is used to treat depression, panic disorder, and OCD. It has a very long half-life, meaning it stays in the body for a long time.
Escitalopram is an 'SSRI' antidepressant. It is a 'cleaner' version of an older drug (citalopram) and is widely prescribed for depression and generalized anxiety disorder, often being well-tolerated.

Venlafaxine is an 'SNRI' (Serotonin-Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitor). It's used to treat depression, anxiety, and panic disorder. It's known for having a very short half-life, which can cause difficult 'discontinuation syndrome' (withdrawal) if a dose is missed.

Duloxetine is an 'SNRI' antidepressant. It's approved to treat not only depression and anxiety but also chronic pain conditions, such as diabetic peripheral neuropathy, fibromyalgia, and chronic low back pain.

Bupropion is an 'atypical antidepressant.' It works by affecting norepinephrine and dopamine (not serotonin). It's often used for depression (Wellbutrin) and is less likely to cause sexual side effects. As Zyban, it's also approved to help people quit smoking.

Mirtazapine is an 'atypical antidepressant.' It's known for having two major side effects: significant sedation (it's very sleep-inducing) and increased appetite/weight gain. For this reason, it's often used in elderly or cancer patients who have depression, insomnia, and low appetite.

Trazodone is an 'atypical antidepressant' that is rarely used as a primary antidepressant anymore because the dose required is extremely sedating. It is now one of the most commonly prescribed medications for 'insomnia' (sleep) at a low, off-label dose.

Lorazepam is a 'benzodiazepine' medication. It's used to treat anxiety, panic attacks, seizures (status epilepticus), and agitation. It works quickly but has a high potential for dependence and addiction.

Alprazolam is a 'benzodiazepine' medication. It's most commonly prescribed for panic disorder and anxiety. It is very fast-acting but also has a very high potential for abuse, dependence, and addiction.
Diazepam is a long-acting 'benzodiazepine.' It's used to treat anxiety, seizures, and muscle spasms. Because it lasts a long time, it's also used to manage the symptoms of acute alcohol withdrawal.
Clonazepam is a long-acting 'benzodiazepine.' It's used to treat seizure disorders (epilepsy) and panic disorder. It has a high potential for dependence.

Zolpidem is a 'Z-drug,' a non-benzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic. It is one of the most common medications prescribed for the short-term treatment of insomnia. It can cause unusual side effects like sleep-walking or sleep-driving.

Methylphenidate is a 'central nervous system stimulant.' It is a first-line medication used to treat ADHD in children and adults. It works by increasing levels of dopamine and norepinephrine in the brain, which improves focus and reduces impulsivity.

Adderall is a 'central nervous system stimulant,' a combination of amphetamine salts. It is a first-line medication for ADHD, working similarly to methylphenidate to improve focus. It has a high potential for abuse and diversion.

Lisdexamfetamine is a 'stimulant' medication for ADHD. It is a 'prodrug' of dextroamphetamine, meaning it is inactive until it is metabolized by the body. This design gives it a smooth, long-lasting effect and is intended to reduce its potential for abuse.

Sumatriptan is a 'triptan' medication. It is *not* a pain reliever. It is an 'abortive' medication used to stop a 'migraine' headache or cluster headache that is already in progress. It works by constricting blood vessels in the brain.

Risperidone is an 'atypical antipsychotic' medication. It's used to treat schizophrenia, bipolar disorder (mania), and irritability associated with autism. It works by balancing dopamine and serotonin.

Olanzapine is an 'atypical antipsychotic' medication used to treat schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. It is very effective but is also well-known for causing significant side effects, especially major weight gain and metabolic syndrome.

Quetiapine is an 'atypical antipsychotic' medication. It's approved for schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and as an add-on for depression. At lower doses, it is very sedating and is often used 'off-label' to treat insomnia.

Aripiprazole is an 'atypical antipsychotic.' It's used for schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and as an 'add-on' treatment for major depressive disorder (MDD) when other antidepressants aren't working well enough.

Gabapentin is an 'anticonvulsant' (anti-seizure) medication. While it's used for epilepsy, it is *far* more commonly prescribed 'off-label' to treat 'neuropathic pain' (nerve pain), such as diabetic neuropathy, shingles pain, or sciatica.
Pregabalin is a 'controlled substance' medication, chemically related to gabapentin. It's an anticonvulsant that is FDA-approved to treat neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, and restless legs syndrome.

Phenytoin is one of the oldest and most effective 'anticonvulsant' medications, used to treat and prevent seizures (epilepsy). It requires very careful dosing and blood monitoring (therapeutic drug monitoring) because it has a 'narrow therapeutic index' and many drug interactions.
